notes: Chapter argument reconstruction: the chapter positions Wycliffe as the 'morning star of the Reformation' -- the first figure whose sustained public collision with Rome (refusal of papal tribute, campaign against the mendicant friars and indulgence-selling, denial that papal absolution/excommunication exceeds a common priest's, three trials that failed to silence him) and whose translation of the Bible into English form the causal bridge from the Waldensian precedent (ch.4) to the Reformation proper and, via his writings, to Huss and Bohemia (ch.6). The claims that actually carry this argument are: Wycliffe's documented conflicts with royal and papal authority; the completion of the first complete English Bible under his direction and its manuscript diffusion; his repeated survivals of attempts to silence or try him (St Paul's riot, Edward III's death, Gregory XI's death and the Schism, natural death by stroke rather than at the stake); and the transmission of his ideas to Hus. Claims about the scale of his following (the Lollard 'half of England' figure, the queen's status as convert), incidental diplomatic/political detail (embassy duration, Gaunt's constitutional title), and the rhetorical claim of Waldensian scriptural exclusivity are coloring and amplification -- they inflate the movement's reach and the drama of Wycliffe's circumstances but are not the props on which the chapter's central claims (first-forerunner status, Bible translation, providential preservation, seeding of Hus) stand. Impact stage scope: 5 confirmed discrepancies scored (GC-05-003, 040, 051, 082, 120), all anachronistic verdicts that survived the defender's Read-only audit as defense_failed. GC-05-075 (revised to supported) and GC-05-107 (downgraded to disputed) are excluded per the task's confirmed-discrepancy list. No new research performed at this stage (0 searches) -- impact assessment is a Read-only synthesis over the defended.json verdicts, the defense arguments already on file, and the chapter text, consistent with budget discipline for the impact stage.

claims.quote: Before the Reformation, there were at times but very few copies of the Bible in existence; but God had not suffered His word to be wholly destroyed.
claims.claim_text: Before the Reformation there were at times only very few copies of the Bible in existence.

verification.rationale: True in the qualified sense that pre-printing Bibles were hand-copied, costly and mostly Latin, so vernacular Scripture was scarce for the laity; the literal 'very few copies in existence' understates the many Vulgate manuscripts held by institutions.
verification.modern_consensus: Latin Vulgate manuscripts were fairly common in institutions; vernacular lay Bibles were rare before the 15th c.

claims.quote: Except among the Waldenses, the word of God had for ages been locked up in languages known only to the learned; but the time had come for the Scriptures to be translated, and given to the people of different lands in their native tongue.

verification.rationale: The absolute 'except among the Waldenses' reflects 19th-c. Protestant exceptionalism; modern scholarship notes other medieval vernacular Scriptures (Old English partials, Gothic/Wulfila, Old Church Slavonic, a complete Czech Bible c.1360).
verification.modern_consensus: Vernacular Scripture was rare but not confined to the Waldenses.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie's History of Protestantism and the Waldensian narrative White drew on cast the Waldenses as the unique guardians of the vernacular Bible.

sources.title: Wycliffe's Bible (earlier vernacular translations)

sources.title: Anne of Bohemia and the Czech Bible of 1360
sources.note: Charles IV commissioned a Czech Bible completed 1360.

defense.argument: Steelman: White qualifies the claim with 'for ages' and 'locked up in languages known only to the learned.' Read naturally, the sentence describes the general medieval condition in which the laity had no living-vernacular Scripture — and the pre-14th-c. renderings the verifier cites are either partial (Anglo-Saxon psalters and gospel portions, Rolle's psalter) or in tongues that had themselves become archaic and learned (Gothic/Wulfila; Old English after the Norman Conquest). Under that reading the Waldensian vernacular Scriptures are a genuinely salient living-vernacular exception, and 'except among the Waldenses' functions as rhetorical emphasis rather than an exhaustive census. But the Old Church Slavonic Bible — a living liturgical vernacular Scripture used by Slavic Christians for centuries — and the complete Czech Bible of c.1360 are real counterexamples the absolute 'except among the Waldenses' cannot absorb. The verifier correctly traces the exceptionalism to Wylie's Waldensian narrative. The defense narrows severity (the core point about lay inaccessibility of Scripture holds) but does not overturn the anachronistic verdict.

impact.reasoning: The claim overstates Waldensian exclusivity where a complete Czech Bible (c.1360) and the long-standing Old Church Slavonic Scriptures are genuine counterexamples, so it materially misrepresents the medieval vernacular-Scripture landscape rather than merely rounding a date. Centrality is high because it feeds directly into the thesis chain's 'faithful persecuted minority as unbroken guardian of truth' link connecting the Waldenses (ch.4) to Wycliffe (ch.5); it is the rhetorical hinge of the chapter's opening paragraph. Counterfactual: delete the sentence, or soften it to 'among the few,' and the chapter's actual argument about Wycliffe -- his conflicts with Rome, the English Bible, his providential survival -- is untouched; the sentence is a scene-setting overture, not a support beam, so load_bearing is false despite its centrality to the book's larger exceptionalist narrative. This is inherited 19th-c. Protestant historiography (Wylie), not invention, which matters for the authorial-method question: White reproduces a period commonplace rather than fabricating a claim from nothing.

defense.argument: Steelman: the embassy itself is genuine — Wycliffe was a royal commissioner to the 1374 Bruges conference with papal envoys — and 'Netherlands' for the Low Countries (Bruges, in Flanders) is accurate 19th-c. usage, which the verifier already grants. The only defective element is duration. Wycliffe's documented participation ran months in 1374; he was not among the commissioners for the 1375–76 rounds, which John of Gaunt led. 'Two years' has no documentary basis and follows the D'Aubigné/Wylie tradition. No natural reading of 'spent two years in the Netherlands' rescues the figure — it is not a rhetorical or synecdochic phrase but a specific quantity. The anachronistic verdict stands; severity is confined to the inflated duration while the substance of the embassy is sound.

impact.reasoning: This is a minor inflation of duration (months stretched to two years) attached to a genuine, well-documented event -- the embassy itself, its purpose, and its radicalizing effect on Wycliffe are all sound, so this is closer to a date/duration slip than a misrepresentation of substance. Centrality is low: the sentence functions as connective biographical tissue explaining how Wycliffe came to know continental corruption firsthand, not as a claim the chapter's larger argument depends on. Counterfactual: correct 'two years' to 'several months' and the chapter's account of Wycliffe's radicalization via Bruges, and everything that follows from it, is completely unaffected -- clearly not load_bearing. Anachronistic here means White followed D'Aubigne/Wylie's inflated figure rather than inventing it, an authorial-method issue (uncritical reliance on secondary historians) rather than authorial invention.

verification.rationale: John of Gaunt was the dominant figure in Richard II's minority but did not hold the formal office of regent; Richard II acceded under continual councils and Gaunt declined a formal regency. 'Regent' follows the 19th-c. narrative rather than the constitutional record.

defense.argument: Strongest steelman: in 19th-c. idiom 'regent' often denoted the de facto leading power during a royal minority, not only the formal constitutional office. Richard II acceded as a child in 1377, and John of Gaunt — Wycliffe's protector — was the realm's most powerful magnate, so 'became regent of the kingdom' can be read as loosely conveying his political pre-eminence. But the loose reading strains against the specific history it describes: government was deliberately vested in continual councils precisely to keep Gaunt from becoming regent, and his standing in the immediate aftermath of Edward III's death was contested and waning (London's hostility, the Good Parliament backlash, the 1377 St Paul's riot directed at him). So both the constitutional term and the implied dominance are shaky. The verifier correctly traces 'regent' to the D'Aubigné/Wylie narrative. The idiom defense lowers severity but does not overturn the anachronistic verdict.

impact.reasoning: Calling Gaunt formal 'regent' misstates a specific constitutional fact -- government was deliberately placed under continual councils to prevent exactly that -- so it materially misrepresents a point of English constitutional history, not a trivial slip. Centrality is moderate: the claim supports the chapter's providential-protection thread (Wycliffe's protector rises to power just as danger peaks), a recurring motif in this chapter, but that thread is far better carried by the death of Gregory XI and the ensuing Schism, which are historically solid. Counterfactual: replace 'regent' with 'the dominant power at court,' and the providential-protection narrative is untouched, since the Schism a few paragraphs later does the real narrative work of explaining Wycliffe's reprieve -- so this is not load_bearing. It reflects the 19th-c. D'Aubigne/Wylie narrative rather than invention, an inherited imprecision about English constitutional detail rather than a fabricated event.

defense.argument: The verifier over-literalized. White's sentence, in context (¶26), reads 'the first English translation of the Bible ever made.' The natural referent of 'the Bible' is the whole Bible, and the Wycliffite Bible is universally recognized — as the verifier's own rationale concedes ('The Wycliffite Bible is the first complete English Bible') — as the first complete English Bible. The pre-Wycliffe renderings the verifier invokes (Anglo-Saxon psalters, gospel portions, Rolle's psalter) are partial and do not constitute a translation 'of the Bible'; they do not falsify 'first English translation of the Bible ever made' under its ordinary meaning. The second prong — that Hereford and Purvey did much of the actual translating — does not contradict White, who credits Wycliffe with the project ('the greatest work of his life was to be the translation,' ¶23), not with penning every verse; the standard scholarly name 'Wycliffe's Bible' still attributes the enterprise to him. Since the claim as written matches the modern consensus on the first complete English Bible, the anachronistic flag rests on a reading the sentence need not bear. Verdict revised to supported.

sources.note: Lollardy a significant minority, not half the nation.

defense.argument: Steelman: 'nearly one half' is not White's own statistical estimate but the substance of a contemporary observation — the hostile chronicler Henry Knighton wrote that one could scarcely meet two people on the road without one being a disciple of Wycliffe ('every second man'). White thus reproduces a period source's impression of how strikingly Lollardy spread, which is a real historical datum about perception, and one could argue the statement reports a contemporary's testimony rather than asserting a modern census. But the sentence presents it as bare fact about actual adherence ('accepted by nearly one half of the people of England'), and as demography it is hyperbole: modern historians treat Lollardy as a substantial minority, not half the nation. The verifier correctly traces the figure to Knighton via White's 19th-c. authorities. No reading makes the numerical claim true, so the anachronistic verdict stands; the defense only relocates the exaggeration from White to her medieval source.

impact.reasoning: This is a specific, quantified statistic presented as fact rather than a vague gloss, and its actual scale (a substantial minority, not half the nation) is significantly overstated -- a false empirical claim, not a rounding error, which earns higher severity than the chapter's other discrepancies. Centrality is moderate: it supports the chapter's picture of Lollardy's reach and thus the plausibility of the transmission to Huss and of the subsequent persecution narrative, but it is not the mechanism by which either the Bible-translation legacy or the Huss connection is established. Counterfactual: replace 'nearly one half' with 'a substantial part,' and the chapter's claims about Wycliffe's lasting influence, the spread of Lollardy, and its seeding of Hus in Bohemia all survive intact -- so this is not load_bearing. As an anachronistic verdict, it traces cleanly to White's source (Knighton via Wylie/D'Aubigne), illustrating the book's habit of inheriting a hostile chronicler's rhetorical hyperbole as demographic fact, a matter of method rather than invention.

sources.title: John Wyclif's Political Philosophy (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.note: Wycliffe drew on FitzRalph, Marsilius, and earlier thinkers.

defense.argument: The verifier read a claim about reforming forerunners as a claim about intellectual influences of any kind. In context (¶45) White frames Wycliffe as arising 'from the obscurity of the Dark Ages... Raised up like John the Baptist... the herald of a new era' — i.e., no prior Reformer or reform movement supplied him a ready-made 'system of reform.' That framing matches the standard designation of Wycliffe as the 'first of the Reformers' / 'morning star,' which this same verification file marks supported (GC-05-115, GC-05-005). White does not deny that Wycliffe had teachers or sources — ¶4 explicitly credits his scholastic, canon-law and civil-law education — so 'from whose work he could shape his system of reform' most naturally means no predecessor Reformer whose reform program he adapted, and under that reading the claim is defensible. The countervailing point is genuine, though: FitzRalph's dominion theology and anti-fraternalism, and Marsilius of Padua on church-state relations, were prior works from which Wycliffe demonstrably shaped components of his own system, so the sentence is not literally true. This is a real interpretive dispute (turning on what 'system of reform' denotes) rather than a clean modern refutation, so the verdict is downgraded from anachronistic to disputed.

new_sources.title: John Wyclif's Political Philosophy (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
new_sources.note: Documents predecessors (FitzRalph, Marsilius) for components of Wycliffe's system — the basis of the residual dispute, not a refutation of the 'no prior Reformer' reading.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: As written, the sentence fails as a fact claim: Wyclif's dominion doctrine and disendowment program are textually traceable to FitzRalph (whom Wyclif cites as 'Armachanus'), with Bradwardine and Grosseteste ('Lincolniensis') also invoked as authorities, defeating the universal negative under its natural reading -- a fact failure, ruled improbable. The frame component (Wyclif as first to weld these materials into an unprecedented, systematic reform program) survives at well-supported, a framing_gap of 3, so the chapter's 'morning star'/originator claim keeps its substance; only the absolute zero-debt phrasing fails, and this is not a simple period-typical echo since the ruling notes the printed absolute exceeds even Wylie, White's own source, who names pre-Wycliffe forerunners. The failure is independent (no chain inheritance): it inflates Wycliffe's discontinuity from his Oxford milieu, but the chapter's real evidentiary weight rests on his documented conflicts with Rome, the Bible translation, and his repeated providential survivals -- none of which this sentence supplies -- so deleting or correcting it to the weakened version leaves the chapter's argument intact and load_bearing is false.

defense.argument: Steelman: 'among the converts' in 19th-c. usage can mean those won over to the cause or to the Bible, short of formal sectarian membership. Richard II's queen, Anne of Bohemia, genuinely favored vernacular Scripture — she owned the Gospels in three languages, and Wycliffe himself publicly praised her for having the gospel in English — so counting her among those won to the reforming/Bible cause has a documentary basis, and the 'Lollard knights' among the nobility are securely attested, making half the claim sound. But 'convert' overstates the queen's case: Anne remained outwardly orthodox (orthodox funeral, no Lollard profession), and modern scholarship classes her as sympathetic, not a Lollard convert. The verifier's anachronistic call on the queen — following the Wylie/D'Aubigné habit of counting her a convert — is correct. The steelman lowers severity (the nobility half is right; the queen was genuinely sympathetic) but does not overturn.

impact.reasoning: Calling Anne of Bohemia a 'convert' rather than a documented sympathizer overstates a specific person's religious position, which is a real misrepresentation of an individual (not merely rhetorical amplification), but her genuine, well-attested interest in vernacular Scripture keeps the error contained rather than fabricated wholesale. Centrality is low: this detail decorates the picture of Lollardy's social reach (alongside GC-05-082's 'half of England') but nothing in the chapter's account of Wycliffe's own conflicts, his Bible translation, or the transmission to Huss depends on the queen's precise status. Counterfactual: remove the sentence entirely, or restate her as 'sympathetic to the gospel,' and the paragraph's larger claim -- that Lollardy reached beyond commoners into the nobility -- still stands on the independently attested Lollard knights, so this is not load_bearing. The error again traces to Wylie/D'Aubigne's habit of counting sympathizers as converts, an inherited overreading rather than an invented episode.

claims.quote: For the first time in the history of England, the stake was decreed against the disciples of the gospel.
claims.claim_text: The persecution of the Lollards marked the first time in the history of England that burning at the stake was decreed against adherents of the gospel.

verification.rationale: The statute De heretico comburendo (1401) first prescribed burning for heresy in England, and the Lollard William Sawtrey (March 1401) was the first burned—the first time the stake was legally decreed against such 'disciples.'

sources.title: De heretico comburendo (1401)
sources.note: 1401 statute first prescribing burning for heresy.

sources.title: William Sawtrey (first burned, 1401)
sources.note: First person burned for heresy in England.

claims.quote: The English monarchs, eager to strengthen their power by securing the support of Rome, did not hesitate to sacrifice the Reformers.
claims.claim_text: English monarchs sacrificed the Lollard reformers in order to strengthen their power by securing the support of Rome.

verification.rationale: The Lancastrian monarchy (Henry IV), needing ecclesiastical legitimation after usurping the throne, allied with the church against the Lollards, passing De heretico comburendo (1401); the causal link between royal interest and persecution is a mainstream interpretation.

sources.title: De heretico comburendo (Lancastrian alliance with the church)
sources.note: Henry IV backed suppression of Lollardy.

claims.quote: Martyrdom succeeded martyrdom. The advocates of truth, proscribed and tortured, could only pour their cries into the ear of the Lord of Sabaoth. Hunted as foes of the church and traitors to the realm, they continued to preach in secret places, finding shelter as best they could in the humble homes of the poor, and often hiding away even in dens and caves.
claims.claim_text: Lollards were repeatedly martyred, proscribed, and tortured in England, and continued to preach in secret, sheltering in poor homes and hiding in dens and caves.

verification.rationale: After 1401 Lollards were persecuted, some burned, and the movement went underground, surviving clandestinely among artisans and the poor into the 15th–16th c.; the general picture is accurate, with the 'dens and caves' as rhetorical amplification.

sources.title: Lollardy (persecution; underground survival)
sources.note: Lollards persecuted and driven underground.

claims.quote: Notwithstanding the rage of persecution, a calm, devout, earnest, patient protest against the prevailing corruption of religious faith continued for centuries to be uttered.
claims.claim_text: Despite persecution, the Lollard protest against the prevailing corruption of religious faith continued in England for centuries.

verification.rationale: Lollardy persisted underground from the early 15th century into the 16th, where it merged with the early English Reformation—so a protest continuing 'for centuries' is broadly accurate.

sources.title: Lollardy (persistence into the 16th century)
sources.note: Lollardy survived into the Reformation era.

claims.quote: Thousands, it is true, terrified by the fury of their persecutors, purchased their freedom at the sacrifice of their faith, and went out of their prisons, clothed in penitents’ robes, to publish their recantation.
claims.claim_text: Thousands of Lollards, terrified by persecution, recanted their faith and left prison clothed in penitents' robes to publish their recantation.

verification.rationale: Forced recantations and public penance (abjuration) of Lollards are well documented, but the specific scale 'thousands' is not securely quantifiable and reflects rhetorical amplification, hence low confidence on the number.

sources.title: Lollardy (abjurations and recantations)
sources.note: Many Lollards recanted under pressure; exact numbers uncertain.

claims.quote: But the number was not small—and among them were men of noble birth as well as the humble and lowly—who bore fearless testimony to the truth in dungeon cells, in “Lollard towers,” and in the midst of torture and flame, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to know “the fellowship of His sufferings.”
claims.claim_text: A considerable number of Lollards, including men of noble birth, bore testimony to their faith in dungeon cells, in "Lollard towers", and under torture and burning.

verification.rationale: Lollards of varied rank (including the knight Sir John Oldcastle) were imprisoned and burned; the 'Lollards' Tower' at Lambeth Palace was a prison associated with them. The description is grounded in the record.

sources.title: Lollardy (Lollard martyrs incl. noble birth; Lollards' Tower)
sources.note: Lollards of noble and humble rank martyred.

claims.quote: By the decree of the Council of Constance, more than forty years after his death his bones were exhumed and publicly burned, and the ashes were thrown into a neighboring brook.
claims.claim_text: By decree of the Council of Constance, Wycliffe's bones were exhumed and publicly burned more than forty years after his death, and the ashes were thrown into a neighboring brook.

verification.rationale: The Council of Constance condemned Wycliffe (1415) and ordered his remains removed and burned; the order (confirmed by Martin V) was executed in 1428—44 years after his 1384 death—with the ashes cast into the River Swift.

sources.title: John Wycliffe (bones burned 1428, ashes into the Swift)
sources.note: Constance decree; bones burned 1428.

claims.quote: “This brook,” says an old writer, “hath conveyed his ashes into Avon, Avon into Severn, Severn into the narrow seas, they into the main ocean. And thus the ashes of Wycliffe are the emblem of his doctrine, which now is dispersed all the world over.”(124)
claims.claim_text: An old writer said of the brook that received Wycliffe's ashes: "This brook hath conveyed his ashes into Avon, Avon into Severn, Severn into the narrow seas, they into the main ocean. And thus the ashes of Wycliffe are the emblem of his doctrine, which now is dispersed all the world over."
claims.extractor_note: Attributed to an unnamed 'old writer'; carries source-ref (124).

verification.rationale: The passage is Thomas Fuller's (Church History of Britain, 17th c.): 'Thus the brook hath conveyed his ashes into Avon, Avon into Severn, Severn into the narrow seas... And thus the ashes of Wickliffe are the emblem of his doctrine, which now is dispersed the world over.' White's rendering matches Fuller closely; 'an old writer' is an accurate attribution.

sources.title: Thomas Fuller quote on Wycliffe's ashes
sources.note: Quote matches Fuller's Church History.

claims.quote: It was through the writings of Wycliffe that John Huss, of Bohemia, was led to renounce many of the errors of Romanism, and to enter upon the work of reform.
claims.claim_text: The writings of Wycliffe led John Huss of Bohemia to renounce many errors of Romanism and enter upon the work of reform.

verification.rationale: Wycliffe's writings, carried to Prague (notably via Jerome of Prague and the England–Bohemia link), deeply shaped Hus; 'Hussism was Wycliffism transplanted into Bohemian soil' is a standard historiographical judgment, though Hus also had independent Bohemian reform roots.

sources.title: Jan Hus (influence of Wycliffe)
sources.note: Hus deeply influenced by Wycliffe's writings.

claims.quote: Thus in these two countries, so widely separated, the seed of truth was sown. From Bohemia the work extended to other lands.
claims.claim_text: From Bohemia the reform work begun under Huss extended to other lands.

verification.rationale: The Bohemian (Hussite) reform movement influenced later reform currents and reached beyond Bohemia (e.g., its impact on the later Reformation and the Unitas Fratrum/Moravians); the general claim of extension to other lands is accurate.

sources.title: Bohemian Reformation (spread and legacy)
sources.note: Hussite reform influenced wider movements.

claims.quote: A divine hand was preparing the way for the Great Reformation.
claims.claim_text: A divine hand was preparing the way for the Great Reformation through the work of Wycliffe and Huss.

verification.rationale: Theological claim of providential preparation; not empirically checkable.
